title: Quiche Lorraine
subtitle: French savory tart with lardons and Gruyère in a cream custard
excerpt: The original quiche: a blind-baked shortcrust case filled with a simple egg and cream custard studded with lardons and melted Gruyère. No onion. No other additions. Makes one 23 cm tart.
sourceNotes: Quiche Lorraine originates in the Lorraine region of eastern France, documented in French household baking and regional cookery records from the nineteenth century. The original form contains lardons (or smoked bacon) in a cream-and-egg custard, without cheese or onion. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), includes the Lorraine tart among the household entrées. The Gruyère addition is a widespread variant documented in twentieth-century French household guides, accepted as the standard modern form.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The blind-baked case can be made a day ahead. The filled tart is best eaten the day it is made. Reheat slices at 160°C fan for 10 minutes.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven. Reduce to 160°C fan for the filled tart

glossaryTerms.definition: A quiche or custard tart filling is done when it has set at the edges but still trembles slightly in the centre when the tin is shaken. The residual heat finishes cooking the centre as the tart cools. A fully set, non-trembling filling when it comes out of the oven means the eggs have curdled slightly — the texture will be grainy rather than smooth. Remove it before the wobble is gone.

content.text: A quiche Lorraine has four elements: pastry, lardons, Gruyère, and custard. Keep the custard ratio simple: two eggs per 100 ml of cream. Bake it at a moderate temperature and take it out while the centre still trembles. The
moment is the hardest thing to judge. Check it at 25 minutes and again every 5 minutes after that.
Make and blind bake the pastry

Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} into the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add 2–3 tbsp cold water and press to a smooth dough. Wrap and chill for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Roll to 3 mm thick and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Blind bake with paper and beans for 15 minutes, then remove beans and paper and bake for 5 more minutes. Remove from the oven and reduce the temperature to 160°C fan.

Fry the {{lardons}} in a dry frying pan until lightly golden. Drain and scatter across the pastry base. Scatter over the {{gruyere}}. Whisk together the {{eggs}}, {{double-cream}}, {{whole-milk}}, and a few gratings of {{nutmeg-whole}}. Season lightly with salt. Pour over the lardons and cheese. Bake at 160°C fan for 30–35 minutes, until the edges are set and the centre still trembles when the tin is shaken. Remove from the oven and rest for 20 minutes before serving.
